citation: Limb outgrowth is initiated by fibroblast growth factor (FGF) 10 secreted by lateral plate mesoderm cells (Fig. 12.9A). Once outgrowth is initiated, bone morphogenetic proteins (BMPs), expressed in ventral ectoderm, induce formation of the AER by signaling through the homeobox gene MSX2.

statement: At what point during embryonic development do the limb buds first become visible as outpocketings on the lateral body wall?
options: End of the third week
End of the fourth week
Middle of the fifth week
End of the sixth week
Beginning of the seventh week
correctOption: End of the fourth week
optionExplanations: The end of the third week is characterized by gastrulation and the beginning of neurulation, but limb buds are not yet visible. The lateral body wall has not yet formed the specific ridges required for limb outgrowth at this stage.

Limb buds first appear at the end of the fourth week as visible outpocketings on the lateral body wall. The forelimb buds typically appear slightly before the hind limb buds, which follow approximately 1 to 2 days later.

By the fifth week, the limbs have already attained the bud stage and are undergoing further growth. The initial appearance of the outpocketings occurs earlier, specifically at the conclusion of the fourth week.

The end of the sixth week is a much later stage where hand- and footplates have already formed and circular constrictions are visible. Morphogenesis is well underway by this time, far past the initial appearance of the buds.

The seventh week is marked by the rotation of the limbs and the beginning of digit separation. The initial formation of the limb buds occurs nearly three weeks prior to these complex morphological changes.
citation: At the end of the fourth week of development, limb buds become visible as outpocketings that form ridges on the lateral body wall (Fig. 12.1A). The forelimb appears first followed by the hind limb 1 to 2 days later. By the fifth week, limbs have attained the bud stage (Fig. 12.1B).

statement: Which embryonic tissue layer provides the mesenchymal core responsible for forming the bones and connective tissues of the developing limbs?
options: Paraxial mesoderm
Intermediate mesoderm
Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Neural crest cells
correctOption: Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm primarily gives rise to somites, which contribute to the axial skeleton and skeletal muscles, but not the primary mesenchymal core of the limb bones. The limb bones themselves are derived from a different mesodermal source.

Intermediate mesoderm is responsible for the development of the urogenital system, including the kidneys and gonads. It does not contribute to the formation of the appendicular skeleton or the connective tissues of the limbs.

The visceral (splanchnic) layer of lateral plate mesoderm contributes to the formation of the wall of the gut tube and the serous membranes. It is the parietal layer, not the visceral layer, that forms the limb structures.

The parietal (somatic) layer of lateral plate mesoderm forms the mesenchymal core of the limb buds. This core eventually differentiates into the bones, ligaments, and various connective tissues that comprise the appendicular skeleton.

While neural crest cells contribute to many structures including peripheral nerves and certain craniofacial bones, they do not form the primary mesenchymal core of the limb buds. The limb bones are mesodermal in origin.
citation: Initially, the limb buds consist of a mesenchymal core derived from the parietal (somatic) layer of lateral plate mesoderm that will form the bones and connective tissues of the limb, covered by a layer of cuboidal ectoderm. Ectoderm at the distal border of the limb thickens and forms the apical ectodermal ridge (AER)

coreConcept: Function of the Apical Ectodermal Ridge (AER)
statement: What is the primary physiological role of the apical ectodermal ridge (AER) in the developing limb bud?
options: Inducing the immediate ossification of the mesenchymal core
Maintaining a population of undifferentiated, proliferating mesenchymal cells
Directing the 90-degree rotation of the limb segments
Initiating the programmed cell death required for digit separation
Forming the epidermis and dermal layers of the limb
correctOption: Maintaining a population of undifferentiated, proliferating mesenchymal cells
optionExplanations: The AER does not induce immediate ossification; in fact, it prevents differentiation in the adjacent zone. Ossification occurs later in development as cells move away from the AER's inhibitory influence.

The AER exerts an inductive influence on the adjacent mesenchyme, maintaining it as the 'undifferentiated zone.' This allows for rapid cell proliferation and continued distal growth of the limb before differentiation begins.

Limb rotation is a complex morphological event occurring in the seventh week and is not the primary function of the AER. The AER is specifically focused on proximodistal outgrowth and maintaining the undifferentiated state of distal mesenchyme.

While cell death in the AER eventually leads to digit separation, its primary role during the growth phase is the maintenance of the undifferentiated zone. Digit separation is a later event involving the fragmentation of the ridge.

The AER is a specialized thickening of the ectoderm, but its primary functional significance is its inductive signaling to the underlying mesenchyme. The general ectoderm covers the limb to form the epidermis, but the AER's role is regulatory.
citation: This ridge exerts an inductive influence on adjacent mesenchyme, causing it to remain as a population of undifferentiated, rapidly proliferating cells, the undifferentiated zone. As the limb grows, cells farther from the influence of the AER begin to differentiate into cartilage and muscle.

statement: In the proximodistal development of the limbs, which structures constitute the 'zeugopod' segment?
options: Humerus and femur
Carpels and tarsals
Radius/ulna and tibia/fibula
Metacarpals and metatarsals
Scapula and pelvic girdle
correctOption: Radius/ulna and tibia/fibula
optionExplanations: The humerus and femur represent the stylopod, which is the most proximal segment of the developing limb. These are the first bones to begin differentiation as the limb grows away from the body wall.
Carpels and tarsals are part of the autopod, which is the most distal segment of the limb. The autopod also includes the metacarpals, metatarsals, and the phalanges of the digits.

The zeugopod is the middle segment of the limb, consisting of the radius and ulna in the upper extremity and the tibia and fibula in the lower extremity. It differentiates after the stylopod but before the autopod.
Metacarpals and metatarsals are distal structures belonging to the autopod. They form within the hand- and footplates following the condensation of mesenchyme into digital rays.

The scapula and pelvic girdle form the limb girdles, which connect the appendicular skeleton to the axial skeleton. They are not classified within the three primary proximodistal segments (stylopod, zeugopod, autopod).
citation: In this manner, development of each limb proceeds proximodistally into its three components: stylopod (humerus and femur), zeugopod (radius/ulna and tibia/fibula), and autopod (carpels, metacarpals, digits/tarsals, digits/metatarsals). In 6-week-old embryos, the terminal portion of the limb buds becomes flattened

statement: During the sixth week of development, what morphological feature separates the newly flattened hand- and footplates from the proximal segment of the limb?
options: The apical ectodermal ridge
A circular constriction
The digital rays
The mesenchymal core
The interdigital necrotic zones
correctOption: A circular constriction
optionExplanations: The apical ectodermal ridge is a histological thickening at the distal tip of the limb bud, not a gross morphological constriction. It regulates growth but does not define the boundary between the plates and the proximal limb.

In 6-week-old embryos, a circular constriction appears that separates the flattened terminal portion (hand- and footplates) from the more proximal segments. This is one of the first visible signs of limb segmentation.

Digital rays are mesenchymal condensations that form within the hand- and footplates to define the future digits. They appear after the plates have already been established and separated by the initial constriction.

The mesenchymal core is the internal tissue of the limb bud that gives rise to bones and connective tissue. It is a structural component rather than a surface morphological feature like a constriction.

Interdigital necrotic zones are areas of programmed cell death that occur later to separate the fingers and toes. They are located within the autopod itself, not at the junction with the proximal limb.
citation: In 6-week-old embryos, the terminal portion of the limb buds becomes flattened to form the hand- and footplates and is separated from the proximal segment by a circular constriction (Fig. 12.1 C). Later, a second constriction divides the proximal portion into two segments

statement: What is the initial event that triggers the separation of the limb's distal extremity into five distinct digits?
options: Condensation of the radius and ulna
Cell death in the apical ectodermal ridge (AER)
Rotation of the limb 90 degrees medially
Secretion of retinoic acid by the flank
Formation of the circular constriction
correctOption: Cell death in the apical ectodermal ridge (AER)
optionExplanations: Condensation of the radius and ulna occurs in the zeugopod, which is proximal to the digits. This process is related to the formation of the forearm/leg bones, not the separation of the fingers.

The formation of fingers and toes begins when programmed cell death occurs within the apical ectodermal ridge (AER), dividing it into five separate segments. Each segment then independently directs the outgrowth of a digit.

Limb rotation occurs in the seventh week and affects the orientation of the entire limb relative to the body axis. It does not cause the separation of the digits, which is a localized process in the autopod.
Retinoic acid is involved in the differentiation of the proximal stylopod near the flank. It is not the primary trigger for the fragmentation of the AER into five distinct digital segments.

The circular constriction separates the hand/footplate from the proximal limb segment. While it defines the autopod, it is the subsequent fragmentation of the AER that initiates the formation of individual digits.
citation: Fingers and toes are formed when cell death in the AER separates this ridge into five parts (Fig. 12.3A). Further formation of the digits depends on their continued outgrowth under the influence of the five segments of ridge ectoderm, condensation of the mesenchyme to form cartilaginous digital rays

statement: During the seventh week of gestation, in which directions do the upper and lower limbs rotate, respectively?
options: Upper limb rotates medially; lower limb rotates laterally
Both limbs rotate 90 degrees laterally
Both limbs rotate 90 degrees medially
Upper limb rotates laterally; lower limb rotates medially
Upper limb rotates 180 degrees; lower limb does not rotate
correctOption: Upper limb rotates laterally; lower limb rotates medially
optionExplanations: This option is the exact opposite of the actual developmental process. The upper limb moves laterally to position the thumb, while the lower limb moves medially to position the big toe.

If both limbs rotated laterally, the lower limb's extensor muscles would be positioned posteriorly and the big toe would be lateral, which does not match the final anatomical position of the human leg.
If both limbs rotated medially, the upper limb's thumb would be medial and the extensor muscles would be anterior, which is the opposite of the standard anatomical position of the arm.

The upper limb rotates 90 degrees laterally, placing the extensor muscles on the posterior/lateral surface and the thumb laterally. Conversely, the lower limb rotates 90 degrees medially, placing the extensors anteriorly.

Limbs do not undergo a 180-degree rotation; a 90-degree shift in opposite directions is sufficient to achieve the characteristic orientation of the human extremities and their respective muscle groups.
citation: Also, during the seventh week of gestation, the limbs rotate in opposite directions. Hie upper limb rotates 90° laterally so that the extensor muscles lie on the lateral and posterior surface, and the thumbs lie laterally, whereas the lower limb rotates approximately 90° medially

coreConcept: Relative timing of upper and lower limb development
statement: How does the timing of lower limb morphogenesis generally compare to that of the upper limb?
options: The lower limb is 1 to 2 days ahead of the upper limb
The lower limb is approximately 1 to 2 days behind the upper limb
The lower limb is exactly 1 week behind the upper limb
Both limbs develop at the exact same time
The lower limb is 5 days behind the upper limb
correctOption: The lower limb is approximately 1 to 2 days behind the upper limb
optionExplanations: The upper limb (forelimb) always appears first in development. The lower limb (hind limb) follows shortly after, so it cannot be ahead of the upper limb in its morphogenesis.

Morphogenesis of the lower limb lags slightly behind the upper limb by approximately 1 to 2 days. This small delay is consistent from the initial appearance of the limb buds through the later stages of differentiation.

A one-week delay is too long; the developmental window for limb formation is relatively tight. The actual lag is only 1 to 2 days, ensuring both sets of limbs are well-formed by the end of the embryonic period.

While the processes are very similar, they are not perfectly simultaneous. The cranio-caudal gradient of development ensures that the upper limbs begin their morphogenesis slightly before the lower limbs.
A five-day delay is an overestimation of the developmental lag. The precise timing described in embryological texts consistently places the lower limb just 1 to 2 days behind the upper limb.
citation: Development of the upper and lower limbs is similar except that morphogenesis of the lower limb is approximately 1 to 2 days behind that of the upper limb. Also, during the seventh week of gestation, the limbs rotate in opposite directions. Hie upper limb rotates 90° laterally

statement: What happens to the mesenchymal cells of the 'undifferentiated zone' as the limb bud grows distally and they are no longer adjacent to the apical ectodermal ridge (AER)?
options: They undergo programmed cell death to form the joints
They begin to differentiate into cartilage and muscle
They migrate back toward the spinal segments
They transform into the cuboidal ectoderm of the limb
They remain permanently undifferentiated to form the bone marrow
correctOption: They begin to differentiate into cartilage and muscle
optionExplanations: Programmed cell death is essential for digit separation and joint cavity formation, but it is not the primary fate of the cells leaving the undifferentiated zone. Most of these cells contribute to the structural tissues of the limb.

As the limb grows, cells that are left behind and are no longer under the inductive influence of the AER begin to differentiate. These cells eventually form the cartilaginous models of the bones and the associated muscle tissue.
Mesenchymal cells in the limb bud do not migrate back toward the spinal segments. Their movement is generally characterized by proliferation and distal expansion as the limb elongates.

The mesenchymal core is derived from mesoderm, while the cuboidal ectoderm is a separate germ layer. Mesenchymal cells cannot transform into ectodermal cells; they differentiate into mesodermal derivatives like bone.

The undifferentiated zone is a transient population of cells required for limb outgrowth. Once they are far enough from the AER, they must differentiate to form the functional tissues of the limb; they do not stay undifferentiated.
citation: The apical ectodermal ridge (AER) at the distal border of the limb regulates proximodistal limb outgrowth by secreting FGFs that maintain a region of rapidly dividing cells immediately adjacent to the ridge called the undifferentiated zone. As the limb grows, cells near the flank are exposed to retinoic acid

coreConcept: Spinal segment association of limb buds
statement: The upper limb buds typically develop adjacent to which specific spinal segments?
options: C1—C4
C5—T2
L2—S2
S3—S5
correctOption: C5—T2
optionExplanations: The C1—C4 segments are located too cranially and are primarily associated with the development of the neck and the innervation of the diaphragm via the phrenic nerve, not the upper limbs.
The upper limb buds form adjacent to the C5 through T2 spinal segments. This positioning is critical as it determines the segmental innervation of the arm by the brachial plexus.
The T3—T12 segments correspond to the thoracic region of the body wall. While they contribute to the intercostal structures, they are not the primary site for limb bud formation.
The L2—S2 segments are the site of formation for the lower limb buds, not the upper limbs. This lower positioning corresponds to the future lumbar and sacral plexuses that innervate the legs.
The S3—S5 segments are located at the most caudal end of the spinal column and are associated with the pelvic floor and perineal structures, rather than the development of the extremities.
citation: Limbs form at the end of the fourth week as buds along the body wall adjacent to specific spinal segments determined by HOX genes (upper limb, C5—T2; lower limb, L2—SZ). The apical ectodermal ridge (AER) at the distal border of the limb regulates proximodistal limb outgrowth

statement: Following the appearance of the first circular constriction, what event allows for the recognition of the three main segments of the extremities?
options: The disappearance of the apical ectodermal ridge
The formation of a second constriction in the proximal portion
The immediate ossification of the digital rays
The 90-degree lateral rotation of the lower limb
The fusion of the parietal and visceral mesoderm
correctOption: The formation of a second constriction in the proximal portion
optionExplanations: The AER does not disappear to allow segmentation; it remains active at the tips of the developing digits to drive their outgrowth. Segmentation is a morphological process involving constrictions.

After the first constriction separates the hand/footplate, a second constriction divides the remaining proximal portion into two distinct segments. This allows the stylopod and zeugopod to be recognized as separate entities.

Ossification is a late process that occurs after the main segments of the limb have already been morphologically defined. The recognition of the segments depends on the external constrictions, not internal bone formation.

Rotation occurs in the seventh week and changes the orientation of the segments, but it is not the process that creates the segments themselves. The segments are defined by the constrictions that appear earlier.
The parietal and visceral layers of the lateral plate mesoderm do not fuse to form limb segments. The limb is derived specifically from the parietal layer, and its segmentation is a patterning event.
citation: In 6-week-old embryos, the terminal portion of the limb buds becomes flattened to form the hand- and footplates and is separated from the proximal segment by a circular constriction (Fig. 12.1 C). Later, a second constriction divides the proximal portion into two segments, and the main parts of the extremities can be recognized (Fig. 12.1D).

statement: Which of the following tissues are primarily formed by the mesenchymal core of the limb bud?
options: Epidermis and hair follicles
Bones and connective tissues
Nerves and Schwann cells
Melanocytes and sensory ganglia
The lining of the gut tube
correctOption: Bones and connective tissues
optionExplanations: The epidermis and hair follicles are derived from the surface ectoderm that covers the limb bud, not from the internal mesenchymal core. The ectoderm provides the outer protective layer.
The mesenchymal core, which originates from the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, is the precursor for the bones, cartilages, ligaments, and other connective tissues of the limb.
Nerves and Schwann cells migrate into the limb bud from the neural tube and neural crest, respectively. They are not derived from the primary mesenchymal core of the lateral plate mesoderm.
Melanocytes and sensory ganglia are derivatives of the neural crest. While they are present in the limb, they do not originate from the mesenchymal core that forms the skeleton.
The lining of the gut tube is derived from the endoderm. The limb buds are located on the lateral body wall and do not contribute to the internal mucosal linings of the visceral organs.
citation: Initially, the limb buds consist of a mesenchymal core derived from the parietal (somatic) layer of lateral plate mesoderm that will form the bones and connective tissues of the limb, covered by a layer of cuboidal ectoderm. Ectoderm at the distal border of the limb thickens and forms the apical ectodermal ridge (AER) (Fig. 12.2).

statement: What process is essential for the final separation of the individual fingers and toes after the digital rays have formed?
options: Hypertrophy of the intervening mesenchyme
Programmed cell death of the tissue between the rays
Lateral rotation of the hand- and footplates
Secretion of mucus by the cuboidal ectoderm
Ossification of the interdigital spaces
correctOption: Programmed cell death of the tissue between the rays
optionExplanations: Hypertrophy (increase in cell size) of the intervening tissue would lead to syndactyly (fused digits) rather than separation. Separation requires the removal of tissue, not its growth.

The final separation of digits is achieved through programmed cell death (apoptosis) of the intervening tissue located between the cartilaginous digital rays. This removes the 'webbing' between the fingers and toes.
Rotation changes the position of the limb relative to the body but does not affect the internal separation of the digits within the autopod. Digit separation is a localized cellular event.
The cuboidal ectoderm does not secrete mucus to separate digits. The process is driven by internal cellular signaling that leads to the breakdown of the mesenchyme in specific zones.

Ossification occurs within the digital rays to form the phalanges. If the interdigital spaces ossified, the digits would be fused into a solid bony plate, which is a pathological rather than a developmental state.
citation: Further formation of the digits depends on their continued outgrowth under the influence of the five segments of ridge ectoderm, condensation of the mesenchyme to form cartilaginous digital rays, and the death of intervening tissue between the rays (Fig. 12.3B,C). Development of the upper and lower limbs is similar except that morphogenesis

statement: As a result of the 90-degree medial rotation of the lower limb, where are the extensor muscles located in the adult anatomical position?
options: Posterior surface
Lateral surface
Anterior surface
Medial surface
Ventral surface
correctOption: Anterior surface
optionExplanations: In the upper limb, the lateral rotation places the extensor muscles on the posterior surface. In the lower limb, the medial rotation results in a different orientation for the homologous muscles.
The lateral surface of the lower limb contains different muscle groups (like the fibularis muscles). The extensors are moved to a more central functional position by the medial rotation.
The 90-degree medial rotation of the lower limb places the extensor muscles on the anterior surface. This explains why the knee extends anteriorly, while the elbow extends posteriorly.
The medial surface of the lower limb is primarily occupied by the adductor group. The extensors are positioned anteriorly following the seventh-week rotation.

Ventral is often used synonymously with anterior in some contexts, but in standard embryological descriptions of limb rotation, 'anterior' is the specific term used to describe the final position of the lower limb extensors.
citation: Hie upper limb rotates 90° laterally so that the extensor muscles lie on the lateral and posterior surface, and the thumbs lie laterally, whereas the lower limb rotates approximately 90° medially, placing the extensor muscles on the anterior surface and the big toe medially. Limb outgrowth is initiated by fibroblast growth factor

statement: Which tissue layer is responsible for initiating the outgrowth of the limb by secreting the primary signaling factors?
options: Surface ectoderm
Neural crest
Lateral plate mesoderm
Paraxial mesoderm
Endoderm
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: While the surface ectoderm eventually forms the AER, it does not initiate the process. The ectoderm responds to signals already being sent by the underlying mesodermal cells.
Neural crest cells migrate into the limb later to form pigment cells and sensory nerves. They are not involved in the initial signaling that triggers the formation of the limb bud from the body wall.

Limb outgrowth is initiated by cells of the lateral plate mesoderm. These cells secrete signaling factors (such as FGF10) that act on the overlying ectoderm to induce the formation of the apical ectodermal ridge.
Paraxial mesoderm forms the somites and eventually the limb muscles, but it is not the source of the initial signals that trigger the outgrowth of the limb bud itself.
The endoderm forms the internal linings of the respiratory and digestive tracts. it has no direct role in the initiation or structural formation of the appendicular skeleton or limb buds.
citation: Limb outgrowth is initiated by fibroblast growth factor (FGF) 10 secreted by lateral plate mesoderm cells (Fig. 12.9A). Once outgrowth is initiated, bone morphogenetic proteins (BMPs), expressed in ventral ectoderm, induce formation of the AER by signaling through the homeobox gene MSX2. Expression of RADICAL FRINGE, a homologue of Drosophila fringe

coreConcept: Embryonic origin of limb connective tissues
statement: Which specific embryonic tissue layer is responsible for the formation of the mesenchymal core that eventually develops into the bones and connective tissues of the limbs?
options: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Paraxial mesoderm (somites)
Intermediate mesoderm
Neural crest cells
correctOption: Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The visceral or splanchnic layer of the lateral plate mesoderm primarily contributes to the development of the gut wall and the serous membranes surrounding organs. It does not participate in the formation of the limb's mesenchymal core or its skeletal elements. The limb buds specifically arise from the somatic layer of the lateral plate mesoderm.

The parietal (somatic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm forms the mesenchymal core of the limb buds, which is the precursor to the appendicular skeleton and associated connective tissues. This layer is covered by ectoderm, which later thickens to form the apical ectodermal ridge. This developmental origin is distinct from the axial skeleton, which arises from paraxial mesoderm.

Paraxial mesoderm, specifically the sclerotome of the somites, is responsible for the formation of the axial skeleton, including the vertebrae and ribs. While somite-derived cells migrate into the limb bud to form skeletal muscle, they do not form the primary mesenchymal core that produces the limb bones. The limb bones themselves are derived from the lateral plate mesoderm.

Intermediate mesoderm is the embryonic source for the urogenital system, including the kidneys and gonads. It does not contribute to the formation of the limb buds or the appendicular skeleton. The limbs are formed from the more lateral somatic layer of the lateral plate mesoderm.

Neural crest cells contribute to various structures including the peripheral nervous system, melanocytes, and craniofacial cartilage. While they provide sensory innervation to the limbs, they are not the source of the mesenchymal core that forms the limb bones and connective tissues. The primary structural components of the limb are mesodermal in origin.
citation: Initially, the limb buds consist of a mesenchymal core derived from the parietal (somatic) layer of lateral plate mesoderm that will form the bones and connective tissues of the limb, covered by a layer of cuboidal ectoderm. Ectoderm at the distal border of the limb thickens and forms the apical ectodermal ridge (AER).

coreConcept: Ectodermal covering of the early limb bud
statement: In the early stages of limb development, what is the histological characteristic of the ectodermal layer that covers the mesenchymal core before the formation of the apical ectodermal ridge?
options: Simple squamous ectoderm
Pseudostratified columnar ectoderm
Cuboidal ectoderm
Stratified squamous ectoderm
Ciliated columnar ectoderm
correctOption: Cuboidal ectoderm
optionExplanations: Simple squamous ectoderm is a very thin layer of flattened cells that is not characteristic of the early limb bud covering. The initial ectoderm is more substantial in shape to support the rapid growth and signaling required for limb initiation. The actual covering is described as cuboidal in nature.

Pseudostratified columnar ectoderm is typically found in the respiratory tract or parts of the male reproductive system. It is not the type of ectoderm found covering the early limb bud. The limb bud ectoderm is a simpler, single layer of cuboidal cells before it thickens distally.

The early limb bud is covered by a layer of cuboidal ectoderm, which serves as the outer boundary for the proliferating mesenchyme. At the distal border, this cuboidal layer thickens to form the apical ectodermal ridge (AER). This ridge is essential for maintaining the underlying mesenchyme in an undifferentiated state.

Stratified squamous ectoderm is a multi-layered epithelium found in the mature skin (epidermis). In the early embryo, the ectoderm has not yet differentiated into this complex stratified structure. The early limb bud covering is a simpler cuboidal layer.

Ciliated columnar ectoderm is specialized for moving fluids or particles across a surface, such as in the uterine tubes or respiratory bronchioles. It is not found on the external surface of the developing embryo or the limb buds. The limb bud ectoderm is cuboidal and non-ciliated.
citation: Initially, the limb buds consist of a mesenchymal core derived from the parietal (somatic) layer of lateral plate mesoderm that will form the bones and connective tissues of the limb, covered by a layer of cuboidal ectoderm. Ectoderm at the distal border of the limb thickens and forms the apical ectodermal ridge (AER).

coreConcept: Timing of the limb bud stage
statement: During which week of embryonic development have the limbs typically attained the formal 'bud stage' following their initial appearance as ridges?
options: Third week
Fourth week
Fifth week
Sixth week
Seventh week
correctOption: Fifth week
optionExplanations: The third week of development is characterized by gastrulation and the formation of the three germ layers. Limb buds have not yet appeared at this stage, as they only begin to form as ridges at the end of the fourth week. The formal bud stage is reached later.

At the end of the fourth week, the limb buds are just becoming visible as outpocketings or ridges on the lateral body wall. While this is the start of the process, they have not yet fully attained the characteristic 'bud stage' described in morphological milestones. That milestone occurs in the subsequent week.

By the fifth week of development, the limbs have progressed from initial ridges to the formal bud stage. This stage follows the initial appearance of the forelimb and hind limb at the end of the fourth week. The bud stage represents a period of rapid mesenchymal proliferation and early proximodistal patterning.

In the sixth week, the limb buds undergo significant morphological changes, such as the flattening of the terminal portions to form hand- and footplates. This is a more advanced stage than the initial bud stage. The bud stage itself is a hallmark of the fifth week.

The seventh week is marked by the rotation of the limbs and the further differentiation of the digits. By this time, the limbs are far beyond the simple bud stage and have recognizable segments. The bud stage is an earlier event occurring in the fifth week.
citation: At the end of the fourth week of development, limb buds become visible as outpocketings that form ridges on the lateral body wall (Fig. 12.1A). The forelimb appears first followed by the hind limb 1 to 2 days later. By the fifth week, limbs have attained the bud stage (Fig. 12.1B).

statement: In the proximodistal patterning of the limbs, which of the following anatomical structures are classified as part of the autopod?
options: Humerus and femur
Radius and ulna
Tibia and fibula
Carpels, metacarpals, and digits
Scapula and pelvic girdle
correctOption: Carpels, metacarpals, and digits
optionExplanations: The humerus and femur are the proximal-most bones of the upper and lower limbs, respectively. They constitute the stylopod segment of the limb. The autopod refers to the most distal segment of the limb.
The radius and ulna are the bones of the forearm, which represent the middle segment of the upper limb. This segment is known as the zeugopod. It is located between the stylopod and the autopod.

The tibia and fibula are the bones of the lower leg, representing the middle segment of the lower limb. Like the radius and ulna, they constitute the zeugopod. They are not part of the distal autopod segment.

The autopod is the most distal component of the limb and includes the carpels, metacarpals, and digits in the upper limb, and the tarsals, metatarsals, and digits in the lower limb. It is the final segment to differentiate during proximodistal growth. This segment forms from the flattened hand- and footplates.

The scapula and pelvic girdle are part of the appendicular skeleton but function as the attachment points for the limbs to the axial skeleton. They are not considered part of the three proximodistal segments (stylopod, zeugopod, autopod) of the limb itself. They form the shoulder and pelvic girdles.
citation: In this manner, development of each limb proceeds proximodistally into its three components: stylopod (humerus and femur), zeugopod (radius/ulna and tibia/fibula), and autopod (carpels, metacarpals, digits/tarsals, digits/metatarsals).

statement: During limb morphogenesis, the middle segment of the limb differentiates into specific skeletal elements. Which of the following pairs correctly identifies the zeugopod components for both the upper and lower limbs?
options: Humerus and femur
Radius/ulna and tibia/fibula
Carpels and tarsals
Metacarpals and metatarsals
Phalanges and digits
correctOption: Radius/ulna and tibia/fibula
optionExplanations: The humerus and femur are the single large bones of the proximal limb segment. They are classified as the stylopod. The zeugopod is the middle segment and contains two parallel bones in each limb.

The zeugopod is the middle segment of the limb, consisting of the radius and ulna in the upper extremity and the tibia and fibula in the lower extremity. These structures differentiate after the stylopod but before the autopod. This patterning follows a strict proximodistal sequence.

Carpels and tarsals are the small bones of the wrist and ankle, respectively. They are part of the autopod, which is the most distal segment of the limb. They are not part of the middle zeugopod segment.

Metacarpals and metatarsals are the bones of the hand and foot that precede the digits. They are components of the autopod. The zeugopod is located more proximally and consists of the forearm and leg bones.

Phalanges are the bones that make up the digits (fingers and toes). They are the most distal elements of the autopod. The zeugopod refers specifically to the middle segment containing the radius/ulna or tibia/fibula.
citation: In this manner, development of each limb proceeds proximodistally into its three components: stylopod (humerus and femur), zeugopod (radius/ulna and tibia/fibula), and autopod (carpels, metacarpals, digits/tarsals, digits/metatarsals).

statement: In the sequence of limb development, what is the specific anatomical result of the second circular constriction that appears after the formation of the hand- and footplates?
options: It separates the digits from the metacarpals.
It divides the proximal portion into the stylopod and zeugopod.
It separates the limb bud from the lateral body wall.
It induces the formation of the apical ectodermal ridge.
It separates the carpels from the radius and ulna.
correctOption: It divides the proximal portion into the stylopod and zeugopod.
optionExplanations: The separation of digits from the metacarpals is a process involving programmed cell death in the apical ectodermal ridge and the intervening tissue, not a circular constriction. The first constriction separates the autopod from the rest of the limb. The second constriction occurs more proximally.

After the first constriction separates the distal hand- or footplate (autopod) from the proximal limb, a second constriction appears. This second constriction divides the remaining proximal segment into two distinct parts: the stylopod (proximal) and the zeugopod (middle). This allows the main parts of the extremities to be morphologically recognized.

The limb bud is already distinct from the lateral body wall by the time these constrictions appear. The constrictions are internal to the limb bud itself and serve to demarcate the three primary segments. They do not define the junction with the trunk.

The apical ectodermal ridge (AER) is formed much earlier, at the end of the fourth week, by the thickening of the distal ectoderm. The constrictions are morphological changes that occur in the sixth week and later. The AER actually influences the growth that leads to these segments.

The separation of the carpels from the radius and ulna is part of the first constriction's effect, which demarcates the autopod from the proximal segments. The second constriction specifically acts on the segment that remains proximal to the autopod. Therefore, it divides the stylopod from the zeugopod.
citation: In 6-week-old embryos, the terminal portion of the limb buds becomes flattened to form the hand- and footplates and is separated from the proximal segment by a circular constriction. Later, a second constriction divides the proximal portion into two segments, and the main parts of the extremities can be recognized.

statement: Which of the following processes is directly responsible for the initial formation of the cartilaginous digital rays within the hand- and footplates?
options: Apoptosis of the apical ectodermal ridge
Condensation of the mesenchyme
Lateral rotation of the limb bud
Hypertrophy of the cuboidal ectoderm
Fragmentation of the parietal mesoderm
correctOption: Condensation of the mesenchyme
optionExplanations: Apoptosis of the apical ectodermal ridge (AER) is the mechanism that separates the ridge into five distinct parts, which then guide digit outgrowth. However, the actual rays themselves are formed from the underlying mesenchyme. Apoptosis alone does not create the cartilaginous structures.

The formation of digital rays occurs when the mesenchyme within the hand- and footplates condenses into five distinct columns. These mesenchymal condensations then chondrify to form the cartilaginous models of the digits. This process is essential for the structural blueprint of the fingers and toes.

Limb rotation occurs in the seventh week and changes the orientation of the entire limb relative to the body. It does not play a role in the internal formation of digital rays or the condensation of mesenchyme. Rotation happens after the basic segments and rays are already established.

While the ectoderm thickens to form the AER, hypertrophy of the cuboidal ectoderm is not the process that forms the digital rays. The ectoderm exerts an inductive influence, but the rays themselves are mesenchymal in origin. The ectoderm remains the outer covering.

The parietal mesoderm is the source of the limb's mesenchymal core, but 'fragmentation' is not the term for digit formation. The process is a localized condensation of existing mesenchyme into rays. Fragmentation would imply a breakdown of tissue rather than organized development.
citation: Further formation of the digits depends on their continued outgrowth under the influence of the five segments of ridge ectoderm, condensation of the mesenchyme to form cartilaginous digital rays, and the death of intervening tissue between the rays.

statement: During the seventh week of gestation, the lower limbs undergo a characteristic rotation. In which direction and by how many degrees do they rotate?
options: 90° laterally
90° medially
180° medially
45° laterally
45° medially
correctOption: 90° medially
optionExplanations: A 90° lateral rotation is characteristic of the upper limb, not the lower limb. This lateral rotation in the upper limb results in the thumb being positioned laterally and the extensor muscles being located posteriorly and laterally. The lower limb rotates in the opposite direction.

The lower limb rotates approximately 90° medially during the seventh week of development. This medial rotation is crucial for the final anatomical positioning of the lower extremity, placing the big toe in a medial position. It also shifts the extensor muscles to the anterior surface of the leg.

A 180° rotation is not observed in normal human limb development. Such a drastic rotation would result in severe malpositioning of the limbs. The standard rotation for both the upper and lower limbs is approximately 90°.

A 45° lateral rotation does not match the developmental milestones described for either limb. The upper limb rotates 90° laterally, and the lower limb rotates 90° medially. These 90° rotations are necessary to achieve the standard anatomical position.

While the lower limb does rotate medially, the degree of rotation is approximately 90°, not 45°. This full quarter-turn is required to bring the hallux (big toe) to the midline and orient the knee and extensor muscles anteriorly. A 45° rotation would be insufficient.
citation: Hie upper limb rotates 90° laterally so that the extensor muscles lie on the lateral and posterior surface, and the thumbs lie laterally, whereas the lower limb rotates approximately 90° medially, placing the extensor muscles on the anterior surface and the big toe medially.

coreConcept: Anatomical result of upper limb rotation
statement: What is the final anatomical orientation of the thumb and the extensor muscles following the 90° lateral rotation of the upper limb?
options: Thumb medially, extensors anteriorly
Thumb laterally, extensors anteriorly
Thumb laterally, extensors laterally and posteriorly
Thumb medially, extensors posteriorly
Thumb posteriorly, extensors medially
correctOption: Thumb laterally, extensors laterally and posteriorly
optionExplanations: The thumb is positioned laterally, not medially, after the upper limb's lateral rotation. Additionally, the extensor muscles of the upper limb are located posteriorly and laterally, whereas anterior extensors are a feature of the lower limb. This option describes a medial rotation which does not occur in the upper limb.

While the thumb does end up in a lateral position, the extensor muscles of the upper limb do not lie on the anterior surface. Anterior extensors are characteristic of the lower limb following its medial rotation. In the upper limb, the anterior surface contains the flexor muscles.

The upper limb rotates 90° laterally during the seventh week. This movement places the thumb (the first digit) in a lateral position and orients the extensor muscles so that they lie on the lateral and posterior surfaces of the limb. This is the standard anatomical position for the upper extremity.

Although the extensor muscles do have a posterior component, the thumb is lateral, not medial. A medial thumb position would only occur if the limb failed to rotate or rotated medially like the lower limb. The lateral rotation is a defining feature of upper limb morphogenesis.

The thumb is oriented laterally in the anatomical position, not posteriorly. Furthermore, the extensor muscles are lateral and posterior, not medial. This configuration does not match the known outcomes of the 90° lateral rotation of the upper limb.
citation: Hie upper limb rotates 90° laterally so that the extensor muscles lie on the lateral and posterior surface, and the thumbs lie laterally, whereas the lower limb rotates approximately 90° medially, placing the extensor muscles on the anterior surface and the big toe medially.

coreConcept: Anatomical result of lower limb rotation
statement: Following the medial rotation of the lower limb during development, where are the extensor muscles and the big toe located relative to the body's midline and surfaces?
options: Extensors on the posterior surface, big toe laterally
Extensors on the anterior surface, big toe medially
Extensors on the lateral surface, big toe medially
Extensors on the anterior surface, big toe laterally
Extensors on the posterior surface, big toe medially
correctOption: Extensors on the anterior surface, big toe medially
optionExplanations: In the lower limb, the extensor muscles are located anteriorly, not posteriorly, after rotation. The big toe is also positioned medially, not laterally. This description more closely resembles the upper limb's posterior extensors, though the thumb is lateral.

The lower limb rotates 90° medially during the seventh week. This results in the extensor muscles being positioned on the anterior surface (e.g., quadriceps, tibialis anterior) and the big toe (hallux) being oriented medially toward the midline. This is the opposite of the upper limb's lateral rotation.

While the big toe is indeed medial, the extensor muscles of the lower limb are primarily on the anterior surface, not the lateral surface. The medial rotation shifts the entire muscle group from its original position to the front of the leg. Lateral muscles in the leg are typically the fibular group.

The extensor muscles are correctly identified as being on the anterior surface, but the big toe is medial, not lateral. A lateral big toe would be a significant malformation or the result of an incorrect lateral rotation. Medial rotation ensures the hallux is the most medial digit.

The big toe is correctly identified as medial, but the extensor muscles are anterior, not posterior. In the lower limb, the posterior compartment contains the flexor muscles (e.g., hamstrings, gastrocnemius). This is a key difference from the upper limb where extensors are posterior.
citation: Hie upper limb rotates 90° laterally so that the extensor muscles lie on the lateral and posterior surface, and the thumbs lie laterally, whereas the lower limb rotates approximately 90° medially, placing the extensor muscles on the anterior surface and the big toe medially.

statement: What is the typical temporal delay in the morphogenesis of the lower limb compared to the upper limb?
options: Lower limb is 1 to 2 days ahead
Lower limb is 1 to 2 days behind
Lower limb is 1 week behind
Lower limb is 2 weeks behind
There is no temporal difference
correctOption: Lower limb is 1 to 2 days behind
optionExplanations: The lower limb does not develop ahead of the upper limb. In human embryology, development generally proceeds in a cranio-caudal direction, meaning structures closer to the head (like the forelimbs) appear and mature slightly earlier than those further down the trunk.

Morphogenesis of the lower limb lags slightly behind the upper limb by approximately 1 to 2 days. This delay is consistent from the initial appearance of the limb buds at the end of the fourth week through the subsequent stages of differentiation and rotation. This is a standard feature of vertebrate limb development.

A one-week delay is too long; the developmental gap between the upper and lower limbs is much narrower. By the time a week has passed, the upper limb has already progressed through significant morphological changes that the lower limb will reach in just a couple of days. The actual delay is only 1-2 days.

A two-week delay would mean the upper limb is significantly more advanced, which is not the case. Both sets of limbs develop nearly concurrently, with only a very slight lag for the hind limbs. The 1-2 day difference is the accepted embryological timeframe.

There is a distinct and measurable temporal difference in the appearance and maturation of the limbs. The forelimb buds always appear first, followed shortly by the hind limb buds. Claiming no difference ignores the cranio-caudal gradient of development.
citation: Development of the upper and lower limbs is similar except that morphogenesis of the lower limb is approximately 1 to 2 days behind that of the upper limb. Also, during the seventh week of gestation, the limbs rotate in opposite directions.

coreConcept: Physical appearance of early limb buds
statement: How do the limb buds first appear on the embryo at the end of the fourth week of development?
options: As small circular pits on the ventral surface
As outpocketings forming ridges on the lateral body wall
As elongated filaments extending from the dorsal midline
As flattened plates on the cranial end
As segmented blocks of mesoderm near the heart
correctOption: As outpocketings forming ridges on the lateral body wall
optionExplanations: Limb buds do not appear as pits; pits are more characteristic of sensory organ development, such as the otic or nasal pits. Limb buds are proliferative outgrowths that project outward from the body wall. They are also located laterally, not ventrally.

At the end of the fourth week, the limb buds first become visible as outpocketings that form ridges along the lateral body wall. These ridges are the result of rapid proliferation of the underlying lateral plate mesoderm. The forelimb ridges appear slightly before the hind limb ridges.

Limb buds are not filamentous and do not arise from the dorsal midline. They are bulky outpocketings of mesenchyme and ectoderm located on the lateral aspects of the embryo. The dorsal midline is the site of neural tube and somite development.

Flattened plates (hand- and footplates) are a later stage of limb development occurring in the sixth week. Initially, the limbs are simple ridges or buds. They do not start as plates, nor are they located at the cranial end of the embryo.

Segmented blocks of mesoderm are somites, which are located paraxially along the neural tube. While somites contribute muscle cells to the limbs, the limb buds themselves are distinct outpocketings of the lateral body wall. They are not described as segmented blocks.
citation: At the end of the fourth week of development, limb buds become visible as outpocketings that form ridges on the lateral body wall (Fig. 12.1A). The forelimb appears first followed by the hind limb 1 to 2 days later.

statement: What is the primary functional role of the 'undifferentiated zone' located immediately adjacent to the apical ectodermal ridge (AER)?
options: To initiate the formation of the spinal nerves
To provide a population of rapidly proliferating cells for distal growth
To differentiate immediately into the cartilaginous stylopod
To undergo apoptosis to separate the digits
To form the cuboidal ectodermal covering of the limb
correctOption: To provide a population of rapidly proliferating cells for distal growth
optionExplanations: The undifferentiated zone is not involved in the initiation of spinal nerves. Spinal nerves grow into the limb bud from the developing spinal cord later in development. The zone's role is strictly related to the mesenchymal growth of the limb itself.

The undifferentiated zone consists of a population of mesenchymal cells that are kept in a state of rapid proliferation by signals from the AER. As the limb grows distally, these cells provide the necessary mass for the limb's expansion. Once cells leave this zone's influence, they begin to differentiate.

Cells in the undifferentiated zone do not differentiate immediately; their defining characteristic is that they remain undifferentiated to allow for growth. Differentiation into the stylopod, zeugopod, and autopod only occurs as cells move further away from the AER's influence. The stylopod is the first to differentiate from cells furthest from the ridge.

Apoptosis to separate the digits occurs within the AER itself and the intervening mesenchymal tissue between the digital rays. The undifferentiated zone is a region of high proliferation and growth, which is the opposite of programmed cell death. It exists much earlier than the final digit separation.

The undifferentiated zone is composed of mesenchyme, not ectoderm. The cuboidal ectoderm is the tissue layer that covers the limb bud and gives rise to the AER. The undifferentiated zone is the mesenchymal tissue located just beneath this ectodermal ridge.
citation: The apical ectodermal ridge (AER) at the distal border of the limb regulates proximodistal limb outgrowth by secreting FGFs that maintain a region of rapidly dividing cells immediately adjacent to the ridge called the undifferentiated zone.

statement: The formation of limb buds is precisely localized along the body wall. According to developmental patterns, which spinal segments are associated with the emergence of the lower limb buds?
options: C5—T2
L2—S2
S1—S5
C1—C4
correctOption: L2—S2
optionExplanations: The segments C5—T2 are associated with the development of the upper limb buds. These cervical and upper thoracic segments correspond to the brachial plexus that innervates the arm. The lower limb arises from much more caudal segments.

T12—L5 is not the correct range for the initial lower limb bud formation. While there is some overlap with the lumbar segments, the specific range identified in embryological texts for the lower limb is L2 through S2 (often written as SZ in some texts).

The lower limb buds form adjacent to the spinal segments L2 through S2 (represented as L2—SZ in the source). This localization is determined by the expression of specific HOX genes along the embryonic axis. This ensures that the limbs develop at the correct level to receive appropriate innervation.

The range S1—S5 is too caudal for the primary origin of the lower limb buds. While the sacral plexus does contribute to lower limb innervation, the buds themselves initiate at the L2 level. S5 is at the very end of the vertebral column and does not define the limb's position.

C1—C4 are the highest cervical segments and are associated with the neck and the diaphragm (via the phrenic nerve). They are far superior to the site of upper limb formation (C5—T2) and have no relation to the lower limb buds.
citation: Limbs form at the end of the fourth week as buds along the body wall adjacent to specific spinal segments determined by HOX genes (upper limb, C5—T2; lower limb, L2—SZ).

statement: Which of the following best describes the sequence and direction of tissue differentiation within the developing limb?
options: Distoproximal: Autopod differentiates first, followed by the stylopod.
Proximodistal: Stylopod differentiates first, followed by the zeugopod and autopod.
Simultaneous: All three segments differentiate at the same time.
Mediolateral: The thumb/big toe differentiate before the fifth digit.
Dorsoventral: Extensor muscles differentiate before flexor muscles.
correctOption: Proximodistal: Stylopod differentiates first, followed by the zeugopod and autopod.
optionExplanations: Limb development does not proceed in a distoproximal direction. If the autopod differentiated first, the hand would form before the arm, which is the opposite of what is observed. The growth and differentiation are driven by the distal AER, pushing development from the shoulder/hip outward.

Limb development follows a strict proximodistal sequence. The stylopod (humerus/femur) is the first to differentiate as its cells are the first to be left behind by the advancing AER. This is followed by the zeugopod and finally the autopod, as the limb continues to extend.

The segments do not differentiate simultaneously. There is a clear temporal lag between the formation of the proximal humerus/femur and the distal digits. This is because differentiation is dependent on the distance of the mesenchymal cells from the inhibitory influence of the AER.

While there is a mediolateral (or preaxial/postaxial) axis in limb development, it is not the primary sequence described for the three main segments (stylopod, zeugopod, autopod). The primary axis of growth and segment appearance is proximodistal. Mediolateral patterning occurs within those segments.

Dorsoventral patterning determines the location of extensors versus flexors, but it does not describe the sequence of the three main limb segments. The question asks for the sequence of the stylopod, zeugopod, and autopod, which is a proximodistal process.
citation: As the limb grows, cells farther from the influence of the AER begin to differentiate into cartilage and muscle. In this manner, development of each limb proceeds proximodistally into its three components: stylopod (humerus and femur), zeugopod (radius/ulna and tibia/fibula), and autopod (carpels, metacarpals, digits/tarsals, digits/metatarsals).

coreConcept: Induction of the Apical Ectodermal Ridge (AER)
statement: Following the initiation of limb outgrowth by FGF10, which of the following molecular mechanisms is responsible for the induction of the apical ectodermal ridge (AER)?
options: BMP expression in ventral ectoderm signaling through MSX2
Retinoic acid signaling from the proximal flank
FGF4 and FGF8 secretion from the undifferentiated zone
RADICAL FRINGE expression in the ventral ectoderm
ENGRAILED-1 signaling through the homeobox gene MSX2
correctOption: BMP expression in ventral ectoderm signaling through MSX2
optionExplanations: Bone morphogenetic proteins (BMPs) are expressed in the ventral ectoderm and are critical for the induction of the AER via the homeobox gene MSX2. This process occurs shortly after FGF10 from the lateral plate mesoderm initiates the initial limb bud outgrowth. Without this BMP-mediated induction, the AER would not form, halting further proximodistal development.
Retinoic acid is involved in the differentiation of the proximal stylopod rather than the initial induction of the AER.
FGF4 and FGF8 are secreted by the AER itself to maintain the undifferentiated zone, not to induce the ridge's formation.
RADICAL FRINGE is expressed in the dorsal half of the limb ectoderm, not the ventral, and serves to restrict the AER's location.
ENGRAILED-1 is expressed in the ventral ectoderm but functions to repress RADICAL FRINGE, thereby assisting in border formation rather than directly signaling through MSX2 for AER induction.
citation: Limb outgrowth is initiated by fibroblast growth factor (FGF) 10 secreted by lateral plate mesoderm cells (Fig. 12.9A). Once outgrowth is initiated, bone morphogenetic proteins (BMPs), expressed in ventral ectoderm, induce formation of the AER by signaling through the homeobox gene MSX2.

coreConcept: Molecular localization of the AER border
statement: The precise localization of the apical ectodermal ridge (AER) at the distal tip of the limb bud is established by the expression of which gene at the interface of RADICAL FRINGE-expressing and non-expressing cells?
options: SERZ (Serrate)
MSX2
ENGRAILED-1
FGF10
Retinoic acid
correctOption: SERZ (Serrate)
optionExplanations: SERZ (a homologue of Drosophila serrate) is induced at the boundary where RADICAL FRINGE expression ends, marking the specific site for AER establishment. This interaction between dorsal and ventral ectodermal signals ensures the ridge is positioned exactly at the distal tip. This molecular border is essential for the subsequent proximodistal outgrowth of the limb.
MSX2 is a homeobox gene involved in the induction of the AER by BMPs, not the specific localization of the border via RADICAL FRINGE.
ENGRAILED-1 is expressed in the ventral ectoderm to repress RADICAL FRINGE, helping to define the border but is not the gene induced at the interface itself.
FGF10 is the initial signal from the lateral plate mesoderm that triggers limb outgrowth before the AER is fully localized.
Retinoic acid is a morphogen that influences the differentiation of proximal limb segments like the stylopod and is not involved in AER border localization.
citation: Expression of RADICAL FRINGE, a homologue of Drosophila fringe, in the dorsal half of the limb ectoderm, restricts the location of the AER to the distal tip of the limbs. This gene induces expression of SERZ, a homologue of Drosophila serrate, at the border between cells that are expressing RADICAL FRINGE and those that are not.

coreConcept: Spinal segment levels for upper limb development
statement: The positioning of the upper limb buds along the lateral body wall is determined by HOX genes and corresponds to which specific spinal segments?
options: C5—T2
C3—C7
L2—S2
C1—C4
correctOption: C5—T2
optionExplanations: The upper limb buds develop adjacent to the C5 through T2 spinal segments, a location specified by the expression patterns of HOX genes. This segmental alignment is crucial for the subsequent innervation of the limb by the brachial plexus. The timing of this appearance is at the end of the fourth week of gestation.
C3—C7 does not accurately represent the full range of spinal segments associated with the upper limb bud formation.
T1—T12 represents the thoracic segments, which are primarily associated with the trunk and ribs rather than the upper limb buds.
L2—S2 (or L2—SZ as per some texts) corresponds to the spinal segments associated with the lower limb buds, not the upper limbs.
C1—C4 corresponds to the upper cervical segments, which are superior to the site of upper limb bud initiation.
citation: Limbs form at the end of the fourth week as buds along the body wall adjacent to specific spinal segments determined by HOX genes (upper limb, C5—T2; lower limb, L2—SZ). The apical ectodermal ridge (AER) at the distal border of the limb regulates proximodistal limb outgrowth by secreting FGFs.

coreConcept: Spinal segment levels for lower limb development
statement: During the fourth week of development, the lower limb buds appear on the lateral body wall adjacent to which specific spinal segments?
options: L2—S2
C5—T2
S3—S5
L1—L5
correctOption: L2—S2
optionExplanations: The lower limb buds are positioned adjacent to the L2 through S2 spinal segments (noted as L2—SZ in some embryological texts). This specific localization is regulated by the HOX gene code, ensuring the limbs develop at the correct craniocaudal level. This alignment also dictates the formation of the lumbosacral plexus for lower limb innervation.
C5—T2 is the segmental level for the upper limb buds, which appear slightly earlier than the lower limb buds.
T10—L1 represents the lower thoracic and upper lumbar transition, which is superior to the primary site of lower limb bud formation.
S3—S5 represents the lower sacral segments, which are distal to the main segments involved in lower limb bud initiation.
L1—L5 covers the lumbar region but misses the sacral segments (S1-S2) that are essential for the lower limb's segmental origin.
citation: Limbs form at the end of the fourth week as buds along the body wall adjacent to specific spinal segments determined by HOX genes (upper limb, C5—T2; lower limb, L2—SZ). The apical ectodermal ridge (AER) at the distal border of the limb regulates proximodistal limb outgrowth.

coreConcept: Role of Retinoic Acid in limb segment differentiation
statement: As the limb bud grows distally, what is the specific role of retinoic acid in the differentiation of the proximodistal limb components?
options: It causes cells near the flank to differentiate into the stylopod
It maintains the undifferentiated zone adjacent to the AER
It induces the formation of the digital rays in the autopod
It triggers the 90-degree lateral rotation of the upper limb
It represses the expression of FGF4 and FGF8 in the AER
correctOption: It causes cells near the flank to differentiate into the stylopod
optionExplanations: Retinoic acid is present near the flank of the embryo and acts on the mesenchymal cells as they move out of the influence of the AER's FGFs. This exposure triggers these cells to differentiate into the stylopod, which includes the humerus in the upper limb and the femur in the lower limb. This represents the first stage of the proximodistal differentiation sequence.
The undifferentiated zone is maintained by FGF4 and FGF8 secreted by the AER, not by retinoic acid.
Digital ray formation in the autopod is driven by mesenchymal condensation under the influence of the segmented AER, occurring much later than stylopod differentiation.
Limb rotation is a gross morphological event occurring in the seventh week and is not directly triggered by the retinoic acid gradient used for segment differentiation.
Retinoic acid does not primarily function by repressing FGFs in the AER; rather, it provides a positional signal for the proximal-most cells of the limb bud.
citation: The apical ectodermal ridge (AER) at the distal border of the limb regulates proximodistal limb outgrowth by secreting FGFs that maintain a region of rapidly dividing cells immediately adjacent to the ridge called the undifferentiated zone. As the limb grows, cells near the flank are exposed to retinoic acid that causes them to differentiate into the stylopod.

statement: In a 6-week-old embryo, what morphological feature characterizes the separation of the hand- and footplates from the more proximal segments of the developing limbs?
options: A circular constriction
The appearance of the olecranon
The formation of the synovial joints
The 90-degree medial rotation
The development of the digital rays
correctOption: A circular constriction
optionExplanations: At 6 weeks, the distal ends of the limb buds flatten into hand- and footplates, and a distinct circular constriction appears. This constriction serves as the first morphological boundary between the future autopod and the more proximal segments of the limb. This is a key milestone in the transition from a simple bud to a segmented extremity.
The olecranon and other specific bony landmarks develop later during ossification and joint formation, not at the 6-week bud stage.
Synovial joints form later through the process of cavitation in the cartilaginous models, well after the initial circular constriction appears.
Limb rotation occurs during the seventh week of gestation, which is after the initial 6-week morphological constrictions have formed.
Digital rays begin to form within the hand- and footplates after the plates themselves have been demarcated by the circular constriction.
citation: In 6-week-old embryos, the terminal portion of the limb buds becomes flattened to form the hand- and footplates and is separated from the proximal segment by a circular constriction (Fig. 12.1 C). Later, a second constriction divides the proximal portion into two segments, and the main parts of the extremities can be recognized.

statement: Following the formation of the hand- and footplates, what developmental event allows for the recognition of the three main segments (stylopod, zeugopod, and autopod) of the extremities?
options: A second constriction dividing the proximal portion
The apoptosis of the entire apical ectodermal ridge
The 180-degree rotation of the limb buds
The fusion of the radius and ulna
The secretion of FGF10 by the ectoderm
correctOption: A second constriction dividing the proximal portion
optionExplanations: After the first constriction separates the autopod, a second constriction appears that divides the remaining proximal mesenchyme into two distinct segments. This second division allows the stylopod (proximal) and zeugopod (middle) to be morphologically distinguished. Together with the autopod, these three segments constitute the major parts of the mature limb.
Apoptosis in the AER occurs in specific regions to separate it into five parts for digit formation, but it does not divide the proximal limb into segments.
The limbs rotate 90 degrees, not 180 degrees, and this rotation occurs after the segments have already been morphologically defined.
The radius and ulna remain separate bones in the zeugopod; their fusion would be a pathological finding rather than a normal developmental segmenting event.
FGF10 is secreted by the lateral plate mesoderm to initiate outgrowth, not by the ectoderm to divide the limb into segments.
citation: In 6-week-old embryos, the terminal portion of the limb buds becomes flattened to form the hand- and footplates and is separated from the proximal segment by a circular constriction (Fig. 12.1 C). Later, a second constriction divides the proximal portion into two segments, and the main parts of the extremities can be recognized.

coreConcept: Lower limb rotation and final orientation
statement: During the seventh week of gestation, the lower limb undergoes a 90-degree medial rotation. What is the resulting anatomical position of the extensor muscles and the big toe?
options: Extensors on the anterior surface and big toe medially
Extensors on the posterior surface and big toe laterally
Extensors on the lateral surface and big toe medially
Extensors on the medial surface and big toe laterally
Extensors on the posterior surface and big toe medially
correctOption: Extensors on the anterior surface and big toe medially
optionExplanations: The 90-degree medial rotation of the lower limb brings the original dorsal surface (containing the extensor muscles) to the anterior position. Simultaneously, this rotation moves the preaxial digit (the big toe) from its initial cranial position to a medial position. This explains why the knee extends anteriorly, unlike the elbow which extends posteriorly.
Extensors on the posterior surface and a lateral big toe would describe a limb that did not rotate or rotated laterally like the upper limb.
While the big toe is medial, the extensor muscles are anterior, not lateral, following the 90-degree medial rotation.
The big toe is medial, not lateral, and the extensors are anterior, not medial, in the final anatomical position of the lower limb.
The extensors are located on the anterior surface of the lower limb, not the posterior surface, following the medial rotation.
citation: Hie upper limb rotates 90° laterally so that the extensor muscles lie on the lateral and posterior surface, and the thumbs lie laterally, whereas the lower limb rotates approximately 90° medially, placing the extensor muscles on the anterior surface and the big toe medially.

coreConcept: Upper limb rotation and final orientation
statement: As the upper limb rotates 90 degrees laterally during development, how are the extensor muscles and the thumb positioned relative to the body?
options: Extensors on the lateral/posterior surface and thumb laterally
Extensors on the anterior surface and thumb medially
Extensors on the medial surface and thumb laterally
Extensors on the posterior surface and thumb medially
Extensors on the anterior surface and thumb laterally
correctOption: Extensors on the lateral/posterior surface and thumb laterally
optionExplanations: The upper limb rotates 90 degrees laterally, which shifts the extensor muscles to the lateral and posterior aspects of the limb. This rotation also places the thumb (the preaxial digit) in a lateral position in the standard anatomical position. This is the opposite direction of the lower limb's rotation.
Extensors on the anterior surface and a medial thumb describe the orientation of the lower limb, not the upper limb.
The extensor muscles are lateral and posterior, not medial, and while the thumb is lateral, the muscle orientation is incorrect in this option.
The thumb is positioned laterally, not medially, following the lateral rotation of the upper limb.
The extensor muscles of the upper limb are located posteriorly and laterally, not on the anterior surface, which contains the flexor muscles.
citation: Hie upper limb rotates 90° laterally so that the extensor muscles lie on the lateral and posterior surface, and the thumbs lie laterally, whereas the lower limb rotates approximately 90° medially, placing the extensor muscles on the anterior surface and the big toe medially.

statement: Following the separation of the AER into five distinct parts, what process occurs within the mesenchyme of the hand- and footplates to initiate the formation of the digits?
options: Condensation of mesenchyme to form cartilaginous digital rays
Apoptosis of the entire mesenchymal core
Transformation of ectoderm into bone
Rotation of the digital plates by 45 degrees
Secretion of retinoic acid by the digital rays
correctOption: Condensation of mesenchyme to form cartilaginous digital rays
optionExplanations: Once the AER is divided into five segments, the underlying mesenchyme begins to condense in five specific areas. These condensations form the cartilaginous digital rays, which serve as the precursors to the bones of the fingers and toes. This process is guided by the inductive signals from the overlying segmented AER.
Apoptosis occurs in the intervening tissue between the digital rays to separate the fingers, but the rays themselves are formed by mesenchymal condensation, not death.
The bones of the digits are derived from the mesenchymal core (lateral plate mesoderm), not from the transformation of the overlying ectoderm.
The digital plates do not undergo a separate 45-degree rotation; the entire limb rotates 90 degrees as a unit during the seventh week.
Retinoic acid is primarily involved in the earlier differentiation of the proximal stylopod, whereas digital ray formation is a later distal event.
citation: Fingers and toes are formed when cell death in the AER separates this ridge into five parts (Fig. 12.3A). Further formation of the digits depends on their continued outgrowth under the influence of the five segments of ridge ectoderm, condensation of the mesenchyme to form cartilaginous digital rays.

statement: What is the primary mechanism that divides the continuous apical ectodermal ridge (AER) into five separate segments to facilitate digit development?
options: Programmed cell death (apoptosis) in the AER
Mechanical stretching of the handplate
Invasion of the AER by vascular endothelial cells
The 90-degree rotation of the limb bud
Secretion of FGF10 by the ventral ectoderm
correctOption: Programmed cell death (apoptosis) in the AER
optionExplanations: The transition from a continuous ridge to five distinct segments is achieved through programmed cell death (apoptosis) within specific regions of the AER. These five remaining segments of ectoderm then provide the necessary signals for the outgrowth of the individual digits. This is a critical step in establishing the pentadactyl (five-fingered) pattern.
Mechanical stretching is not the biological mechanism responsible for the precise segmentation of the AER into five parts.
Vascularization occurs within the mesenchymal core of the limb, but it is not the cause of the AER's division into segments.
Limb rotation occurs after the AER has already begun the process of digit patterning and does not cause the ridge to separate.
FGF10 is secreted by the lateral plate mesoderm to initiate the limb bud, not by the ectoderm to divide the AER.
citation: Fingers and toes are formed when cell death in the AER separates this ridge into five parts (Fig. 12.3A). Further formation of the digits depends on their continued outgrowth under the influence of the five segments of ridge ectoderm, condensation of the mesenchyme to form cartilaginous digital rays.

coreConcept: Embryological origin of limb core tissues
statement: The bones and connective tissues of the developing limbs are derived from which specific embryonic tissue layer?
options: Parietal (somatic) layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Visceral (splanchnic) layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Paraxial mesoderm (somites)
Neural crest cells
Endoderm of the lateral body wall
correctOption: Parietal (somatic) layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The mesenchymal core of the limb bud, which eventually differentiates into the skeleton and connective tissues, originates from the parietal (somatic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This layer proliferates to form the initial bulge of the limb bud under the ectoderm. While muscles migrate from somites, the structural framework is lateral plate-derived.
The visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm contributes to the wall of the gut tube and the heart, not the appendicular skeleton.
Paraxial mesoderm (somites) provides the myogenic precursor cells that migrate into the limb to form muscles, but it does not form the bones or general connective tissues.
Neural crest cells contribute to peripheral nerves and melanocytes within the limb but do not form the primary mesenchymal core of the bones.
The endoderm does not contribute to the structure of the limbs; it forms the epithelial lining of the respiratory and digestive tracts.
citation: Initially, the limb buds consist of a mesenchymal core derived from the parietal (somatic) layer of lateral plate mesoderm that will form the bones and connective tissues of the limb, covered by a layer of cuboidal ectoderm. Ectoderm at the distal border of the limb thickens.

coreConcept: Timing difference between upper and lower limb development
statement: In human embryonic development, how does the timing of lower limb morphogenesis typically compare to that of the upper limb?
options: It lags behind the upper limb by 1 to 2 days
It precedes the upper limb by 1 to 2 days
It occurs simultaneously with the upper limb
It lags behind the upper limb by exactly one week
It precedes the upper limb by exactly one week
correctOption: It lags behind the upper limb by 1 to 2 days
optionExplanations: Morphogenesis of the lower limb follows a nearly identical pattern to the upper limb but is consistently delayed by approximately 1 to 2 days. This lag is evident from the first appearance of the limb buds at the end of the fourth week through the formation of the digits. This cranio-caudal progression is a common theme in vertebrate embryology.
The lower limb never precedes the upper limb; the developmental wave moves from the cranial (upper) to the caudal (lower) regions.
While the processes are similar, they are not perfectly simultaneous, as the upper limb bud always appears and differentiates slightly earlier.
A one-week lag is an overestimation; the actual developmental difference is much shorter, typically only 24 to 48 hours.
The upper limb always develops first, so the lower limb cannot precede it by a week or any other duration.
